themes.title: Human–AI Reasoning and Deliberation
themes.desc:
We study how AI systems can participate in structured reasoning with people instead of only producing final answers. This line covers multi-agent discussion, moderation, real-time summaries, argument maps, critical-question generation, and hybrid workflows where people can inspect, challenge, and revise reasoning while it unfolds.

Keywords
Human–AI interactionDeliberationMulti-agent systemsReasoning supportArgument mapsCritical questions
Why it matters

For contested or high-stakes questions, users should be able to inspect, challenge, and shape the reasoning process. We therefore focus on systems that keep people meaningfully inside the loop.

Representative publications
  • ARGSBASE: A Multi-Agent Interface for Structured Human–AI Deliberation (2026) — a real-time deliberation interface with moderator, deliberator, and analyzer roles.
  • Hybrid Intelligence for Logical Fallacy Detection (2025) — combines AI assistance with human reasoning in fallacy analysis.
  • TriLLaMa at CQs-Gen 2025: A Two-Stage LLM-Based System for Critical Question Generation (2025) — explores critical-question generation for argument evaluation.

themes.title: Computational Argumentation
themes.desc:
This is the group’s core line of work on how arguments are expressed, structured, retrieved, and evaluated in natural language. It spans argument components and relations, evidence types, persuasion strategies, deliberative moves, causality, and large-scale argument search across editorials, discussions, and the open web.

Keywords
Argument miningPersuasionArgument searchDeliberative strategiesEvidence typesCausality
Why it matters

Arguments are central to public discourse, education, and information access. Modeling their structure supports analysis, retrieval, and comparison of viewpoints across editorials, discussions, and social media.

Representative publications
  • A News Editorial Corpus for Mining Argumentation Strategies (2016) — introduced a widely used editorial corpus and a fine-grained view of argumentation strategies.
  • Building an Argument Search Engine for the Web (2017) — helped establish large-scale argument retrieval on the web.
  • Modeling Deliberative Argumentation Strategies on Wikipedia (2018) — analyzed deliberative strategies in collaborative online discussion.
  • Analyzing Persuasion Strategies of Debaters on Social Media (2022) — examined how persuasive tactics appear in user-generated debate.

themes.title: Critical Thinking and AI
themes.desc:
This theme asks whether AI can help people reason more carefully rather than simply sound more fluent. We study systems that identify fallacies, generate useful critical questions, compare alternatives, and make bias, stance imbalance, and audience effects easier to notice and discuss.

Keywords
Critical thinkingFallacy detectionCritical questionsBias and balanceStance analysisReflective AI use
Why it matters

Fluent output is not enough. We need language technologies that help people reason more carefully, question assumptions, and stay actively involved in judgment-heavy tasks.

Representative publications
  • Hybrid Intelligence for Logical Fallacy Detection (2025) — studies human–AI collaboration for recognizing fallacious reasoning.
  • TriLLaMa at CQs-Gen 2025: A Two-Stage LLM-Based System for Critical Question Generation (2025) — operationalizes critical questions as a tool for reasoning support.
  • Improving Argument Effectiveness Across Ideologies using Instruction-tuned Large Language Models (2024) — examines how argument improvement interacts with ideological differences.
  • Differential Bias: On the Perceptibility of Stance Imbalance in Argumentation (2022) — investigates how stance imbalance is perceived in argumentative content.

themes.title: Discourse, Narratives, and Structured Language Technologies
themes.desc:
We examine how discourse organization, narrative, framing, and structure shape communication, then build representations and tools that make these patterns computationally useful. This includes narrative use in online discussions and Arabic discourse, argumentation knowledge graphs, controllable generation, and structure-aware exploration tools for complex text collections.

Keywords
NarrativesKnowledge graphsGenerationDiscourse analysisFramingArabic discourse
Why it matters

Many language technologies still treat text as flat output. Structured representations make claims, evidence, issues, and narrative patterns more explicit, interpretable, and reusable in downstream systems.

Representative publications
  • Storytelling in Argumentative Discussions: Exploring the Use of Narratives in ChangeMyView (2025) — analyzes how narratives function inside argumentative discussion.
  • Integrating Argumentation Features for Enhanced Propaganda Detection in Arabic Narratives on the Israeli War on Gaza (2025) — connects narrative and argumentation analysis in Arabic discourse.
  • End-to-End Argumentation Knowledge Graph Construction (2020) — introduced an end-to-end pipeline for building argumentation knowledge graphs.
  • Employing Argumentation Knowledge Graphs for Neural Argument Generation (2021) — used structured argumentative representations to guide generation.

projects.title: AKASE: Argumentation Knowledge-Graphs for Advanced Search Engines (2024–2026)
projects.desc:
AKASE developed argumentation knowledge graphs that encode structured, multi-perspective arguments for advanced search settings. In collaboration with OpenWebSearch.EU, the project used large-scale open web data to support more balanced retrieval, richer justification, and search interfaces that surface credible arguments across perspectives. The project was completed in 2026.

Funding

Funded as an OpenWebSearch.EU third-party project.

Focus

Argumentation knowledge graphs, multi-perspective search, justification, and reasoning-aware search interfaces.

Why it matters

It helps move from flat document retrieval to richer access to arguments, perspectives, and explicit support for reasoning.

projects.title: Narratives in Argumentation (2024–2028)
projects.desc:
This project explores the relationship between narratives and argumentation in persuasive communication. It examines how storytelling patterns, argumentative schemes, and discourse organization interact in online discussions and other forms of public reasoning.

Funding

Funded by the University of Groningen.

Focus

Narrative structure, argumentative schemes, persuasion, annotation, and pattern analysis.

Why it matters

It helps explain how stories and arguments work together in persuasion, deliberation, and human–AI communication.

projects.title: Cross-domain Arabic Argument Mining (2025–2026)
projects.desc:
This project develops resources and methods for Arabic argument mining across domains such as debate, editorials, and public discourse, with attention to claims, evidence, and cross-domain generalization.

Funding

Funded by QatarDebate.

Focus

Annotation, dataset creation, cross-domain modeling, and broader multilingual coverage for argumentation research.

Why it matters

It supports more inclusive language technology and opens new directions for Arabic and cross-domain argument analysis.

thesisTopics.title: Possible thesis directions
thesisTopics.desc:
We regularly supervise bachelor’s and master’s theses connected to ongoing group research.

Examples
Argument miningHuman–AI deliberationCritical thinking supportNarrative analysisStructured generationKnowledge graphs
How topics are shaped

Topics are scoped around the student’s background, level, and interests, and can involve annotation, modeling, evaluation, or interface design.

thesisTopics.title: Working with the group
thesisTopics.desc:
We welcome students who are curious about language, reasoning, AI, and society.

What we value

Clear thinking, methodological care, and a willingness to connect technical work with broader questions about communication and human judgment.

Good starting point

Contact us early with your interests so a topic can be developed in a way that is both feasible and intellectually strong.
